params.description: Scale Preview System from Warmup appears. Student's data visible. All four scale options (1, 2, 5, 10) showing checkmarks.

beats.text: In Warmup, all four scales worked for your data. You had options!

elements.description: Scale Preview System from Warmup. Student's data with all four scales showing checkmarks.

beats.text: But what happens when the numbers are bigger? Let's find out.

elements.description: Scale Preview System from Warmup. Student's data with all four scales showing checkmarks.

params.description: Data table appears. Books Read This Month. Aisha: 20, Ben: 35, Carlos: 55, Dana: 80.
Vertical bar graph appears. Books Read This Month. Axis 0-50. Scale of 5 pre-selected. Graph initially empty.

beats.text: Here's data about how many books four students read this month. Dana read the most—80 books!

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Books Read This Month. Axis 0-50. Scale of 5 selected. Graph empty.

beats.text: Let me try Scale of 5 and see what happens.

params.description: Preview generates. Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55) bars appear normally. Dana's bar (80) extends past the graph boundary. Warning indicator appears.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Aisha, Ben, Carlos bars visible. Dana's bar extends past top. Warning indicator showing.

beats.text: Hmm. Carlos's bar is just above my graph, but Dana's bar goes past the top. The graph only goes up to 50 right now.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Aisha, Ben, Carlos bars visible. Dana's bar extends past top. Warning indicator showing.

beats.text: I could make the graph taller to fit 80. Let's see... I'd need 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80...

params.description: Axis extends upward. Tick marks appear: 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80. Axis now crowded with many tick marks.
Graph axis now extends to 80. All bars visible. Dana's bar reaches 80.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Axis 0-80 with 17 tick marks visible (0, 5, 10, 15... 75, 80). All bars visible. Dana's bar at 80.

beats.text: That's a lot of numbers on the axis. 0, 5, 10, 15, 20... all the way up to 80. That's 17 tick marks!

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Axis 0-80. All 17 tick marks highlighted. All bars visible.

beats.text: It works... but is there a better way? When one scale needs too many tick marks, try a bigger scale.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Axis 0-80 with 17 tick marks. All bars visible.

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Books Read data. Mystery=35, Fantasy=55, Science=20, History=75. Current scale is 5, showing 17 tick marks (0, 5, 10...85). Scale Preview System interactive, Scale of 5 currently selected.

beats.text: Scale of 5 works, but that's a lot of tick marks. Let's try a bigger scale.
Click 'Scale of 10' to see what happens.

params.description: Graph updates to Scale of 10. Axis now shows 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80—only 9 tick marks. All four bars fit cleanly. Checkmark indicator appears.

beats.text: Look at that—only 9 tick marks now instead of 17. Much cleaner!
All the data fits, and the graph is easier to read.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph with Scale of 10. Books Read data. Mystery=35, Fantasy=55, Science=20, History=75. Axis shows 9 tick marks (0, 10, 20...80). Checkmark indicator visible.
Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Books Read data. Scale Preview System interactive. Scale of 5 currently selected showing 17 tick marks. click_scale_button tool active.

params.description: Data table appears showing data values. Largest value (80) is highlighted.

beats.text: Here's the first thing to check: What's your BIGGEST number? That tells you what scales might work.
What's the biggest number in this data?
80 is the biggest. Always check the biggest number first—does it fit on the scale without too many lines on the axis?

elements.description: Data table showing data values. Largest value (80) highlighted.

beats.text: You saw that Scale of 5 needed 17 tick marks. Scale of 10 only needs 9. For big data, bigger scales make cleaner graphs.
When more than one scale fits, pick the one that's easier to read.

elements.description: Data table showing data values. Largest value (80) highlighted.

params.description: Data table appears. Marbles in Jars context. Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.
Horizontal bar graph frame appears with scale selector showing all four scale buttons. No bars displayed yet.

beats.text: Here's data about marbles. Look at the biggest number: 23. That's pretty small!

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars values.
Horizontal bar graph frame with scale selector. Four scale buttons visible. No bars displayed yet.

beats.text: This graph is horizontal — the bars go sideways. Let's check: which scales fit? Try clicking each scale.
Click each scale to see if 23 fits.

validator.description: Student explored all four scales. All fit.

beats.text: All four scales fit!

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars values.
Horizontal bar graph with scale selector. All four scale options previewed, all showing fit indicator.

beats.text: Let's look at Scale of 1.
Click Scale of 1 to see what it looks like.

params.description: Bar graph displays with Scale of 1. All bars fit. Axis shows 24 tick marks (0 through 23).

beats.text: It works — every number lands exactly. But look at all those lines on the side! Small data means all scales fit. But some are cleaner than others.

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars values.
Horizontal bar graph. Scale of 1. Bars for Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23. Axis displays 24 tick marks (0-23).

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Marbles data. Jar A=7, Jar B=24, Jar C=19. Scale of 1 active. 24 tick marks visible on vertical axis.
Data table showing Marbles values alongside graph.

beats.text: Scale of 1 works, but that's a lot of lines. You learned bigger scales are usually easier to read.
Let's try Scale of 10.
Click 'Scale of 10' to compare.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph. Scale of 1 active. Marbles data. 24 tick marks visible.
Data table showing Marbles values.

warnings.message: 7 won't land exactly on a line or in the middle
19 won't land exactly on a line or in the middle

params.description: Scale changes to 10. Axis shows 0, 10, 20, 30. Bars appear. Jar A (7) between 0 and 10. Jar C (19) between 10 and 20. ⚠️ indicators on Jar A and Jar C.

beats.text: Hmm. Scale of 10 fits, but there are only 3 marks on the axis. Look at those warnings. Can you tell the exact value of where those bars end? Someone looking at our graph without the data table would need to guess how many marbles are shown.

For big data, we'd pick Scale of 10 anyway. It's the easiest to read because we don't want too many lines on the axis. But these numbers are small and it's hard to find the exact location of the bar on the graph. We can do better.
When data is small, you have room to find a scale where everything lands exactly.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph. Scale of 10 active. Marbles data. Axis shows 0, 10, 20, 30. Jar A and Jar C have ⚠️ indicators showing values don't land exactly.
Data table showing Marbles values.

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Marbles data. Red=7, Blue=12, Green=9, Yellow=5. Currently showing Scale of 10. Scale selector interactive.

beats.text: Let's try a smaller scale. Try a Scale of 2.
Click 'Scale of 2' to preview.

params.description: Scale transitions to 2. All bars land exactly on tick marks. Green checkmark indicator appears. No warnings.

beats.text: Look—we can read the end of the bars clearly. ALL the numbers land exactly. 7 is right at 7, exactly halfway between 6 and 8. 12 is right at 12.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph showing Scale of 2. Marbles data. All bars land exactly on tick marks. Green checkmark visible.
Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Marbles data showing Scale of 10. Scale selector active with Scale of 2 option available.

beats.text: With Scale of 2, half of 2 is 1. So you can show ANY whole number exactly.
Scale of 2 works to show the exact value for all numbers—and for small data, the graph won't have too many marks on the axis.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph showing Scale of 2. Marbles data. All bars land exactly on tick marks.

params.description: Data Set A displayed on left side. Values: 20, 35, 55, 80. Label: 'Ones digits: 0 or 5'.
Data Set B displayed on right side. Values: 7, 12, 19, 23. Label: 'Ones digits: 7, 2, 9, 3'.

beats.text: Here's a quick way to know if you need Scale of 2. Check the last digit of each number in the ones place.
Numbers that end in 0 or 5—like 20, 35, 55, 80—those are multiples of 5 and can be shown clearly with Scales of 5 and 10.

But look at 7, 12, 19, 23. See the digits in the ones place? 7, 2, 9, 3. None are 0 or 5—that tells you they aren't multiples of 5 so Scale of 2 might be your best choice. Check our groups. Which data set has last digits that are NOT 0 or 5?
Which data set has last digits that are NOT 0 or 5?

validator.description: Student selected Data Set B (7, 12, 19, 23), correct

beats.text: Right. Look at those last digits: 7, 2, 9, 3. None are 0 or 5—that's your Scale of 2 signal.

elements.description: Data Set A on left. Values: 20, 35, 55, 80. Label: 'Ones digits: 0 or 5'.
Data Set B on right. Values: 7, 12, 19, 23. Label: 'Ones digits: 7, 2, 9, 3'.

params.title: Points Scored
params.description: Vertical data table appears. Points Scored data. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

beats.text: Here's data from a game. Four rounds of points: 22, 15, 8, 31.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored data. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

beats.text: Check the ones place digits: 2, 5, 8, 1. Do they all end in 0 or 5?

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored data. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

beats.text: 22... no. 15... yes! 8... no. 31... no. Three of them don't so they aren't multiples of 5. Which scale will show ALL values exactly?
Which scale will show ALL values exactly?

params.title: Points Scored
params.description: Vertical bar graph appears with scale of 2. Points Scored data. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31. All bars fit well, exact values clear.

beats.text: Scale of 2. All of the bars fit well on the graph and their exact values are clear.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored data. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

params.title: Points Scored
params.description: Second vertical bar graph appears. Scale of 1. Same Points Scored data. Side-by-side comparison with Scale of 2 graph. Same height bars, only axis markings differ.

beats.text: Scale of 1 also shows the exact values, but it would require a lot of marks on the axis to get to the greatest value of 31. Let's consider when we might choose a scale of 1.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored data. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.
Vertical bar graph with Scale of 2. Points Scored data.
Vertical bar graph with Scale of 1. Points Scored data. Side-by-side with Scale of 2 graph.

params.description: Vertical data table appears. Stickers Earned Today. Mia: 9, Noah: 5, Olivia: 7, Pete: 3.
Vertical bar graph appears. Stickers Earned Today. Scale Preview System available. No bars showing yet.

beats.text: Look at these numbers: 9, 5, 7, 3. They're really small!

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today values.
Vertical bar graph. Stickers Earned Today. Scale Preview System available. No bars showing.

beats.text: What happens if we try Scale of 10?
Click Scale of 10.
Click 'Scale of 10'

params.description: Scale of 10 applied. Tick marks at 0 and 10. Bars appear with warning indicators.

beats.text: The bars fit, but we don't know the exact value for any of the bars.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today values.
Vertical bar graph with Scale of 10. Tick marks at 0 and 10. Bars showing with warning indicators.
Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today values.
Vertical bar graph. Scale Preview System active. Student selecting scale option.

beats.text: Think about the numbers: 9, 5, 7, 3. They don't end in 0 or 5 so we know Scale of 2 is clearer to show any number. Click Scale of 2.
Click 'Scale of 2'

params.description: Scale of 2 applied. All bars show check indicator. Clean graph with 0 to 10 range.

beats.text: That's better! We can see the bars end at lines or exactly halfway. Scale of 2 could work.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today values.
Vertical bar graph with Scale of 2. All bars show check indicator. Clean graph with 0 to 10 range.
Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today values.
Vertical bar graph. Scale Preview System active. Student selecting scale option.

beats.text: But when numbers are this small, you have another option. Try Scale of 1. What do you notice?
Click Scale of 1.
Click 'Scale of 1'

params.description: Scale of 1 applied. All bars land exactly on tick marks. Check indicator showing. Clean graph with 0 to 10 range.

beats.text: Every number lands right on a line. No halfway bars needed. We can read the graph accurately without having the data table.
For very small numbers, Scale of 1 can be a great choice: simple and exact.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today values.
Vertical bar graph with Scale of 1. All bars land exactly on tick marks. Check indicator showing. Clean graph with 0 to 10 range.
Vertical data table showing Stickers Earned Today values.
Vertical bar graph. Scale Preview System active. Student selecting scale option.

params.description: Vertical data table appears. Favorite Fruits data: Dates 60, Cherries 45, Bananas 30, Apples 15.
Horizontal bar graph placeholder with scale preview system available. All three scales (2, 5, 10) show checkmarks indicating they are valid options.

beats.text: Here's data about students' favorite fruits. The most votes went to Dates with 60. Scale of 1 doesn't make sense here because it would take 60 lines on the axis. That's too much!

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Favorite Fruits data: Dates 60, Cherries 45, Bananas 30, Apples 15.
Horizontal bar graph with scale preview system. All three scales (2, 5, 10) show checkmarks.

beats.text: Your job: pick the right scale. Which scale will you use?
Which scale will you use?
Good choice.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Favorite Fruits data: Dates 60, Cherries 45, Bananas 30, Apples 15.
Horizontal bar graph with scale preview system. Student has selected a scale option.

beats.text: Why is Scale of 10 a good choice? Choose all of the reasons.
Why is Scale of 10 a good choice? Choose all of the reasons.
options: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

and.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

validator.description: Student selected all four options, correct

beats.text: Exactly. All values are multiples of 5, so scales of 5 and 10 both work to read the graph clearly. For data going up to 60, Scale of 10 gives you a cleaner graph.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Favorite Fruits data: Dates 60, Cherries 45, Bananas 30, Apples 15.
Horizontal bar graph with scale preview system. Student has completed scale selection and justification.

params.description: Vertical data table appears. Minutes of Practice data: Guitar 27, Piano 14, Violin 41, Drums 33.

beats.text: Here's data about how many minutes students practiced their instruments.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Minutes of Practice: Guitar 27, Piano 14, Violin 41, Drums 33.

beats.text: Check: are these numbers multiples of 5? Which scale is best for this data?
Which scale is best for this data?
Scale of 2. You saw that 14, 27, 33, 41 aren't multiples of 5 so we need scale of 2 to graph them precisely. Scale of 2 can work when the largest value is 41.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Minutes of Practice: Guitar 27, Piano 14, Violin 41, Drums 33.

params.description: Vertical bar graph creation tool appears with Scale of 2 active. Minutes of Practice context. Gridlines visible at each tick mark. All bars at 0, ready for student to set heights.

beats.text: Now create the bar graph.
Set the bar heights for all 4 categories.

validator.description: Student set all 4 bars to correct heights: Guitar 27, Piano 14, Violin 41, Drums 33

beats.text: You chose the right scale and created the graph. Every bar lands exactly where it should.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Minutes of Practice: Guitar 27, Piano 14, Violin 41, Drums 33.
Vertical bar graph with all 4 bars correctly placed: Guitar 27, Piano 14, Violin 41, Drums 33. Scale of 2. Gridlines visible.

params.description: Data table appears showing Team Scores. Red Team: 25, Green Team: 55, Yellow Team: 70, Blue Team: 40.

beats.text: Last one! Here are scores from four teams. Yellow Team won with 70 points.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores. Red Team: 25, Green Team: 55, Yellow Team: 70, Blue Team: 40.

beats.text: You've done this vertical and horizontal now. Same process every time: check the numbers, choose the scale.
Which scale works best for this data?

validator.description: Student selected scale of 10, most efficient for this data

beats.text: Good choice. All values end in 0 or 5, and the biggest is 70. Scale of 10 gives you a clean graph.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores.

params.description: Horizontal bar graph creation tool appears with scale of 10. Axis shows tick marks at 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80. Empty bars ready for student to set heights.

beats.text: Now create the graph. Place each bar at the right length.
Click to place the bar for Red Team (25 points).

validator.description: Student set Red Team bar to 25

params.description: Red Team bar locks at 25.

beats.text: Right at 25, halfway between 20 and 30.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores.
Horizontal bar graph with scale of 10. Red Team bar set at 25. Other bars empty.

beats.text: Click to place the bar for Blue Team (40 points).

validator.description: Student set Blue Team bar to 40

params.description: Blue Team bar locks at 40.

beats.text: Right on 40.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores.
Horizontal bar graph with scale of 10. Red Team at 25, Blue Team at 40. Green Team and Yellow Team bars empty.

beats.text: Click to place the bar for Green Team (55 points).

validator.description: Student set Green Team bar to 55

params.description: Green Team bar locks at 55.

beats.text: 55, halfway between 50 and 60.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores.
Horizontal bar graph with scale of 10. Red Team at 25, Blue Team at 40, Green Team at 55. Yellow Team bar empty.

beats.text: Click to place the bar for Yellow Team (70 points).

validator.description: Student set Yellow Team bar to 70

params.description: Yellow Team bar locks at 70.

beats.text: Right on 70.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores.
Horizontal bar graph with scale of 10. All bars complete. Red Team at 25, Blue Team at 40, Green Team at 55, Yellow Team at 70.

beats.text: That's it. You chose the scale and placed every bar exactly where it should be, some exactly on the lines and some halfway between.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores.
Complete horizontal bar graph with scale of 10. Red Team at 25, Blue Team at 40, Green Team at 55, Yellow Team at 70.

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Favorite Colors data from previous sections.

beats.text: You learned to choose scales. Check the biggest number. Does it fit without too many marks on the axis? Check the ones place digits. Are they 0 or 5 to find the exact place for the bar? For very small data, scale of 1 could work. Then pick the easiest to read.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph showing Favorite Colors data.

params.description: Bar graph clears from screen.

beats.text: Let's see what you know.
